text: The surgeon begins the gastric bypass procedure along the lesser curvature, creating a window into the lesser sac. Using the EndoWrist stapler, the surgeon forms the gastric pouch over a visible forty French dilator, which helps calibrate the pouch size. The stapler's wristed function allows for precise angling during pouch creation. Seam guards are applied to parts of the pouch, although they can shift and require repositioning. The robotic stapler's intelligent sensing aids in achieving secure staple formation, and the pouch is completed without seam guards at the top of the staple line.
The surgeon identifies the transverse colon and uses a vessel sealer to divide the omentum, clearing the path for an anti-colic gastric bypass.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to open a gastrotomy in the gastric pouch, creating an opening while sealing the edges. The endoverous stapler is employed to create a side-to-side anastomosis, positioning the small bowel to the pouch. The third robotic arm assists by holding and positioning tissues during the procedure.

An NG tube is placed across the anastomosis site, and the surgeon begins closing the enterotomy left by the stapler using absorbable 2-0 VLOC suture. The suture is applied in a running full-thickness manner to close the defect, followed by serosal stitches to bury the initial suture line, creating a second line in an efficient manner.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to stabilize the bowel while creating enterotomies for the jejuno-jejunostomy using a vessel sealer. The vessel sealer cuts and seals the tissue, requiring the surgeon to stretch the opening manually.

The surgeon uses a white load wristed stapler to create a side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy, extending the anastomosis to approximately 90 millimeters by taking a second firing to ensure adequate length and prevent narrowing.

The surgeon closes the enterotomy in the small bowel using an absorbable 2-0 V-lock suture, starting with a running full thickness suture along the staple line. A silk crotch stitch is placed at the end of the staple line. Upon reaching the top of the enterotomy, the surgeon reverses direction to apply a second layer of serosal stitches, effectively burying the first suture line.

The surgeon uses a single suture to create a tight, secure suture line along the mesentery, closing the mesentery defect efficiently. The third arm holds the mesentery in position, allowing the stitches to be fired across smoothly, completing a two-layer closure on the enterotomy and mesentery in one continuous action.
